vulnerabilities.description: MIME type validation relies on client-provided Content-Type header which can be spoofed. Although Sharp validation mitigates this by processing the actual image, the check at line 19 should be more defensive.
SQL filtering performed client-side after fetching all rows instead of server-side WHERE clauses. Inefficient and leaks data structure to authenticated users.
Batch reordering endpoint doesn't validate that IDs exist before updating. Missing records silently fail to update.
Same batch reordering issue - IDs in order array aren't validated before update operations.
Original filename stored and returned, which could leak information about uploaded files. Use only randomized filename in responses.
Database path defaults to './local.db' in current working directory if DB_PATH unset. Could be accidentally committed or exposed in backups.
Database credentials stored as environment variable. While this is standard practice, ensure .env.local is gitignored.

recommendations: Move MIME validation to occur after Sharp processes the image, not before - catch both spoofed headers and corrupted files
Convert client-side filtering to server-side SQL WHERE clauses in projects/route.ts (lines 21-31) for both efficiency and security
Add existence validation before batch updates - query IDs first, then update only confirmed records
Return only randomized filename in media responses, not originalName
Set DB_PATH explicitly in deployment; never rely on current working directory default
Add rate limiting to file upload endpoint to prevent abuse
Consider adding Content-Security-Policy headers to prevent XSS from user-stored content (journal, project descriptions)
The auth layer is well-designed (bcrypt hashing, JWT sessions) - maintain this standard
